'''Maurie Fa'asavalu''' (born 12 January 1980) is a former professional [[rugby union]] and [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 2000s and 2010s. He has played representative level rugby union (RU) for [[Samoa national rugby union team|Samoa]], and at club level for [[Taula Apia Rugby Union]], [[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]] and [[Oyonnax Rugby|Oyonnax]], and representative level rugby league (RL) for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] and [[England national rugby league team|England]], and at club level for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. He was selected for the Great Britain national rugby league team squad in 2007, after living in England for 4 years. He returned to rugby union in October 2010, having signed for [[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]] for the [[2010–11 English Premiership (rugby union)|2010–11 English Premiership]] season.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.planetrugby.com/story/0,25883,16024_6263266,00.html|title=Quins lure international back to Union|publisher=planetrugby.com|date=16 July 2010|access-date=16 July 2010}}</ref> He then transferred to [[Oyonnax Rugby|Oyonnax]] in France. More recently he has joined West Park St Helens as player/ coach.

==Rugby union career==
Fa'asavalu played for [[Samoa national rugby union team|Samoa]] at the [[2003 Rugby World Cup]].

He was signed by the then St. Helens coach [[Ian Millward]] in the winter of 2003 after a series of outstanding performances playing at flanker for Samoa in the [[2003 Rugby World Cup]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Samoans make switch |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/st_helens/3372973.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=22 January 2026 |date=6 January 2004}}</ref> On his return to rugby union, he also featured in the [[2011 Rugby World Cup]].

[[Harlequin F.C.|Harlequins]] managed to lure the [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] (RL), and [[Samoa national rugby union team|Samoan]] (RU) international back to rugby union. "A player of Maurie's talent and ability will be a great asset to Quins," said Quins' Director of Rugby Conor O'Shea, "I have no doubt that he will be able to make an immediate impact on returning to union. He will bring experience and a new dimension to our options and is exactly the type of person we want at this club." He started for Harlequins in their [[2011–12 English Premiership (rugby union)|2011–12 English Premiership]] Final victory over [[Leicester Tigers]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/18154802|title=Harlequins 30-23 Leicester|publisher=BBC Sport|date=26 May 2012|access-date=2 March 2016}}</ref>

On 4 April 2014, Maurie signed for French side [[Oyonnax Rugby|Oyonnax]] who compete in the [[Top 14]] on a two-year contract.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.phoenixsm.com/abendanon-secures-dream-clermont-move|publisher=Phoenix SM|title=Oyonnax sign Samoa back-row Maurie Fa'asavalu from Harlequins|date=4 April 2014}}</ref>

On 4 Oct 2021 it was reported that he had turned out for [[West Park St Helens]] in their local derby win over [[Liverpool St Helens F.C.|Liverpool St Helens]].<ref>{{cite web|website=St Helens Star|date=4 Oct 2021|accessdate=13 Oct 2021|title=Former Saints dual code international Maurie Fa'asavalu plays in West Park's win over Liverpool St Helens|url=https://www.sthelensstar.co.uk/sport/19623425.former-saints-dual-code-international-maurie-faasavalu-plays-west-parks-win-liverpool-st-helens/}}</ref> As of 2024, he was still at the club as a coach.<ref>{{cite web |title=Playing rugby was a free passport to travel – Maurie Fa’asavalu |url=https://www.therugbypaper.co.uk/features/442794/playing-rugby-was-a-free-passport-to-travel-3/ |website=The Rugby Paper |access-date=22 January 2026 |date=29 December 2024}}</ref>

==Rugby league career==
===St Helens===
Fa'asavalu made his début on 13 March 2004 against [[Leeds Rhinos]] in a 24 – 14 victory at [[Knowsley Road]]. Fa'asavalu took some time to adjust to [[rugby league]], but developed into an important part of the St. Helens squad in 2006. He was however used primarily as an impact player, starting most games from the interchange bench. Fas'asavalu was unlucky not to receive the Lance Todd Trophy for his performance in the [[2006 Challenge Cup]] Final victory, where he scored a try in the 2006 and his performance against [[Huddersfield Giants]] earned widespread praise.<ref>{{cite web |title=Huddersfield 12-42 St Helens |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/5271814.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=22 January 2026 |date=26 August 2006}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Dave Woods Cup final verdict |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/5289776.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=22 January 2026 |date=26 August 2006}}</ref> St. Helens fans were treated to Fa'asavalu's victory dance in both the [[2006 Challenge Cup]] Final and the Grand Final for 2006's [[Super League XI]]. His block busting runs and inimitable [[try celebration]]s saw him develop a real following on the terraces at Knowsley Road. In total he scored 10 tries in 28 games during the 2006 season. St Helens reached the [[2006 Super League Grand final]] to be contested against [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]], and Fa'asavalu played from the interchange bench in Saints' 26–4 victory. This was followed by 9 tries in 30 games in the 2007 season. He played in 2007's [[Super League XIII#Grand Final|Super League XIII Grand Final]], 2008's [[Super League XIII#Grand Final|Super League XIII Grand Final]], and 2009's [[Super League XIV#Grand Final|Super League XIV Grand Final]] defeats all against the [[Leeds Rhinos]].<ref name="2008 Grand Final">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7642170.stm|title=2008 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=4 October 2008|access-date=5 October 2008}}</ref> 

He played in the [[2009 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref>{{cite news|last=Fletcher|first=Paul|title=St Helens 10-18 Leeds Rhinos|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm|access-date=22 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=10 October 2009}}</ref>

He missed a major part of the 2010 season after being injured. His last game for St. Helens was in the home victory over Leeds Rhinos.

===International===
On 27 October 2007, Fa'asavalu scored on his début for Great Britain against New Zealand in Huddersfield, helping the British team to a 20–14 victory.<ref>{{cite web |title=First Test - G Britain 20-16 N Zealand |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7063581.stm |website=BBC Sport |access-date=22 January 2026 |date=27 October 2007}}</ref> Five minutes after he came on as a substitute in the 18th minute, Fa'asavalu used his great stature to break through 3 New Zealand players to score the try.

Fa'asavalu was selected for the [[England national rugby league team|England squad]] to compete in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] tournament in Australia.<ref name="Purdham earns World Cup call-up">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7656553.stm|title=Purdham earns World Cup call-up|publisher=BBC|date=7 October 2008|access-date=7 October 2008}}</ref> [[2008 Rugby League World Cup Group A|Group A]]'s first match against [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]] he played from the interchange bench in England's victory. He played 3 games for the England team in the World Cup, in the process breaking his hand in the defeat against Australia.
